outbound_official.note: No official Belgium government outbound visa-list page located. Belgium's FPS Foreign Affairs (diplomatie.belgium.be) publishes per-country travel advice pages for Belgian citizens (e.g. diplomatie.belgium.be/fr/pays/[country]) but maintains no single consolidated list of visa-free, visa-on-arrival, e-visa, or ETA destinations for Belgian ordinary passport holders comparable to India's MEA VFFIN page. Rely on destination-side data.

name: Belgium

visa_free.notes: EU/EEA citizens have freedom of movement in Belgium and the Schengen area; no visa required for any duration. They may enter with a valid national identity card or passport.

eta.nationality: All Annex II (Regulation (EU) 2018/1806) visa-exempt third-country nationals
eta.notes: ETIAS (European Travel Information and Authorisation System) will be required for all visa-exempt third-country nationals travelling to Schengen states including Belgium. ETIAS is expected to become operational in Q4 2026; the exact mandatory enforcement date will be announced at least 6 months before launch. The fee is EUR 20 (confirmed by European Commission announcement of 17 July 2025; up from the originally planned EUR 7). Children under 18 and adults over 70 are exempt from the fee. ETIAS is valid for 3 years or until passport expiry (whichever is earlier), and permits stays of up to 90 days within any 180-day period. EU/EEA and Swiss nationals are exempt from ETIAS. Not yet operational as of June 2026. Note: The EU Entry/Exit System (EES) became fully operational on 10 April 2026, recording biometric data of non-EU nationals at Schengen borders; EES is separate from ETIAS and applies to all non-EU/EEA nationals regardless of visa-free status.

visa_policy.notes: Belgium is part of the Schengen Area. Visa policy for short stays (up to 90 days in any 180-day period) is governed by EU Regulation (EU) 2018/1806 (consolidated version 03.02.2025; December 2025 version also reviewed). All EU/EEA citizens have freedom of movement with no stay limit. Vanuatu was permanently moved to Annex I (visa required) by Regulation (EU) 2025/11, effective 14 January 2025. Kosovo became visa-free from 1 January 2024 via Regulation (EU) 2023/850. No new countries were added to or removed from Annex II in 2025-2026 beyond the Vanuatu removal. ETIAS authorization will be required for all visa-exempt nationals once launched (expected Q4 2026; exact mandatory enforcement date to be announced). The EU Entry/Exit System (EES) became fully operational on 10 April 2026. Belgium grants visa exemption only to persons travelling on refugee passports (travel documents) issued by the United States of America, on the condition that they have legal long-term residence in the US; refugee travel documents from Canada, Australia, UK, or other countries do NOT qualify for Belgian visa exemption.

conditional_access.conditions: Third-country nationals holding a valid long-stay visa (national long-stay visa, more than 90 days) issued by any Schengen Member State may travel visa-free to Belgium for up to 90 days within any 180-day period. Five identity parameters must match 100% between travel document and visa: name, first name, sex, date of birth, nationality. For stays exceeding 90 days in Belgium specifically, a Belgian residence permit or Belgian D visa is always required.

Third-country nationals holding a valid residence permit issued by any Schengen Member State may travel visa-free to Belgium for up to 90 days within any 180-day period. Five identity parameters must match 100% between travel document and permit: name, first name, sex, date of birth, nationality. Certain French temporary receipts (récépissé) and German Aufenthaltstitel without specific endorsements lack visa-free value. Cyprus and Ireland residence permits in principle have no Schengen visa-free value except those explicitly issued to family members of EU citizens under Directive 2004/38. UK residence cards and visas carry no Schengen visa-free value.

credential.subtype: residence permit issued to EU citizen family member under Directive 2004/38

conditional_access.conditions: Irish residence permits are generally excluded from Schengen visa-free travel. Exception: permits that explicitly state they were issued to a family member of an EU citizen or issued pursuant to Directive 2004/38 may grant visa-free access. This exception covers residence permits only - never visas. Each document must be individually verified against the official IBZ list.

credential.subtype: residence permit issued to EU citizen family member under Directive 2004/38

conditional_access.conditions: Cypriot residence permits are generally excluded from Schengen visa-free travel. Exception: permits that explicitly state they were issued to a family member of an EU citizen or issued pursuant to Directive 2004/38 may grant visa-free access. This exception covers residence permits only - never visas. Each document must be individually verified against the official IBZ list.

Belgium grants visa exemption only to persons travelling on refugee passports (travel documents) issued by the United States of America, on the condition that they have legal long-term residence in the US. Refugee or stateless person travel documents issued by Canada, Australia, UK, or other countries do NOT qualify for Belgian visa exemption. Source: EU Commission national derogations document (section 2.2.1 - BNL column) and IBZ exceptions page.

Bilateral visa waiver agreement. Applies to holders of diplomatic, service/official, and special passports. Belgium (BNL) applies this in harmonisation with Benelux partners.
Bilateral visa waiver agreement. Applies to holders of diplomatic, service/official, and special passports. Belgium (BNL) applies this in harmonisation with Benelux partners.

Applies to holders of diplomatic and service/official passports. Belgium (BNL) applies this in harmonisation with Benelux partners per Convention of 11 April 1960.

Applies to diplomatic, service/official, and special passport holders. Belgium (BNL) applies this in harmonisation with Benelux partners per Convention of 11 April 1960.

cbi.notes: Belgium does not operate a dedicated Citizenship-by-Investment program. No official Belgian government source lists a CBI scheme. Naturalization is possible after 5 years of lawful residence (including via investor/entrepreneur routes), subject to language proficiency in French, Dutch, or German and demonstrated social and economic integration. The Belgian Immigration Office (IBZ) and Federal Public Service Foreign Affairs websites confirm no direct CBI pathway.

programs.name: Professional Card for Self-Employed / Entrepreneur
programs.notes: Third-country nationals wishing to conduct self-employed activities in Belgium for more than 90 days must obtain a Professional Card from the competent regional authority (Flanders, Wallonia, Brussels-Capital, or German-speaking Community). There is no official fixed minimum investment amount. After 5 years of lawful residence, applicants may apply for long-term resident status. Application for the D visa is made at the Belgian embassy/consulate, supported by the regional Professional Card decision.

programs.name: Single Permit (Salaried Employee)
programs.notes: Combined work and residence permit for non-EU nationals employed more than 90 days in Belgium (since 3 January 2019). Employer applies on behalf of the worker to the relevant regional authority. Region makes work authorization decision; Immigration Office decides on residence. Standard decision period: 4 months from admissibility (extendable). After 5 years of lawful and uninterrupted residence, applicants may obtain long-term resident status (L card). EU Blue Card holders benefit from combined EU residency calculation for long-term resident purposes.

programs.name: Long-Term Resident Status (EC Long-Term Residence Permit)
programs.notes: Requires 5 years of legal and uninterrupted residence in Belgium. Financial condition: stable, regular and sufficient means of subsistence (minimum EUR 1,038/month for single person, plus EUR 346 per dependent). Medical insurance required. Must not pose danger to public order. Study and vocational training periods count at 50%. EU Blue Card holders may combine EU-wide residency (minimum 2 years immediately before application in Belgium). Grants L card (EU long-term residence permit). Processing: Immigration Office has 5 months to decide.

programs.name: EU Blue Card (Highly Qualified Worker)
programs.eligibility: Third-country nationals with higher education qualifications or high professional skills, employed by a Belgian employer under a Single Permit application. Identified with code B29 on the visa/permit.
programs.processing_time: 4 months from admissibility (extendable); region makes work authorization decision while Immigration Office decides residence
programs.notes: EU Blue Card holders benefit from family reunification without proof of sufficient means of subsistence. Can combine EU-wide residency across Schengen states for long-term resident status (minimum 2 years in Belgium immediately before application). Applied via Single Permit procedure by employer.

programs.name: Researcher Permit
programs.eligibility: Third-country nationals conducting scientific research in Belgium under a hosting agreement with a recognized research institution.
programs.notes: Listed as a separate category on the IBZ work themes page. Requires hosting agreement with recognized research organization. Long-term mobility option also available for researchers already authorized in another EU member state.

programs.name: Intra-Corporate Transfer (ICT) Permit
programs.eligibility: Managers, specialists, or trainee employees being transferred within a multinational enterprise to a Belgian entity for more than 90 days.
programs.notes: Listed as 'Worker ICT' category on the IBZ work themes page. Separate procedure from the standard Single Permit.

programs.name: Orientation Year After Higher Studies
programs.eligibility: Third-country national graduates of Belgian higher education institutions who wish to seek employment or start a business after completing their studies.
programs.notes: Allows graduates a period to look for work or start self-employed activity in Belgium before converting to a work or entrepreneur permit.

agreements: Benelux Convention on short-stay visas (11 April 1960, Article 3) - harmonised Benelux visa policy for diplomatic passport exemptions
EU-Cape Verde Visa Facilitation Agreement (OJ L 282, 24.10.2013)
EU Regulation 2023/850 - Kosovo visa liberalisation (effective 1 January 2024)
EU Regulation 2025/11 - Vanuatu transferred to Annex I (effective 14 January 2025)

validation_notes: ADDED Kosovo (XKX) to visa_free: visa-free from 1 January 2024 per EU Regulation 2023/850 with biometric passport - was absent from prior record.

ADDED Albania (ALB), Bosnia and Herzegovina (BIH), Georgia (GEO), Moldova (MDA), Montenegro (MNE), North Macedonia (MKD), Serbia (SRB), Ukraine (UKR) to visa_free: all are in Annex II of EU Regulation 2018/1806 and were entirely absent from the prior record.
ADDED Nauru (NRU) to visa_free: listed in Annex II of EU Regulation 2018/1806, was absent from prior record.
ADDED Taiwan (TWN), Hong Kong SAR, Macao SAR to visa_free: all listed in Annex II (under 'Other Entities' and 'Special Administrative Regions' sections), were absent from prior record.

CONFIRMED Vanuatu correctly absent from visa_free: Regulation (EU) 2025/11 permanently moved Vanuatu to Annex I (visa required), effective 14 January 2025. Prior record's notes referenced a temporary suspension; updated to permanent removal.

CORRECTED diplomatic/service passport conditional_access: prior record was significantly incomplete (missing Algeria, Cambodia, Congo, Cote d'Ivoire, Cuba, Egypt, Gabon, Gambia, Ghana, Guinea, India, Iran, Jordan, Mongolia, Oman, Philippines, Qatar) and had wrong passport type data for several countries (Bolivia, Cambodia, China, Ecuador, Indonesia were missing 'special' type; Kazakhstan, Jamaica had wrong type data). Updated to match EUR-Lex 2023XC0927(01) BNL table.

REMOVED Armenia, Azerbaijan, Belarus from conditional_access diplomatic passport entries: these countries appear in the EU visa facilitation agreement context but were listed with EU-Armenia/Azerbaijan/Belarus framework references - the EUR-Lex 2023XC0927 table fetched does not show them under BNL column; removed to avoid incorrect attribution. Flagged as gap.
CORRECTED China entry in conditional_access: changed from 'diplomatic only' to 'diplomatic, service, special' per EUR-Lex derogations table.
CORRECTED Bolivia entry in conditional_access: changed from 'diplomatic, service' to 'diplomatic, service, special' per EUR-Lex derogations table.
CORRECTED Indonesia entry in conditional_access: changed from 'diplomatic, service' to 'diplomatic, service, special' per EUR-Lex derogations table.
CORRECTED Kazakhstan entry in conditional_access: changed from 'diplomatic only' to 'diplomatic, service' per EUR-Lex derogations table.
CORRECTED Pakistan entry in conditional_access: confirmed as 'diplomatic, service' (prior had 'diplomatic only').
CORRECTED Senegal entry in conditional_access: changed from 'diplomatic only' to 'diplomatic, service' per EUR-Lex derogations table.
ADDED India to conditional_access with diplomatic, service passports per EUR-Lex derogations table - was absent from prior record.
UPDATED ETIAS note: clarified that ETIAS is expected operational Q4 2026 with mandatory enforcement from approximately April 2027 and full strict enforcement from approximately October 2027.
CORRECTED ETIAS fee: updated from EUR 7 to EUR 20 per European Commission official announcement of 17 July 2025 (home-affairs.ec.europa.eu). Fee exemptions: under-18 and over-70 travellers.

NOTED Georgia diplomatic/service/official passport suspension: European Commission suspended visa-free travel for Georgian holders of diplomatic, service, and official passports effective 6 March 2026 until 6 March 2027 (extendable). Ordinary Georgian passport holders remain visa-free - visa_policy.visa_free Georgia entry unchanged.
CONFIRMED Fiji NOT in Annex II: the December 2025 consolidated text (CELEX:02018R1806-20251230) confirms Fiji is in Annex I (visa required), not Annex II. Fiji correctly absent from visa_free list.

CONFIRMED EU Regulation 2018/1806 December 2025 consolidated version (02018R1806-20251230) reviewed: no additional countries added to or removed from Annex II beyond what is already recorded (Vanuatu removed January 2025, Kosovo added January 2024 via M3).

UPDATED ETIAS note (2026-06-29): removed unconfirmed 'April 2027' and 'October 2027' enforcement milestones that appeared in prior revision notes but are not confirmed in current official EC sources. Current official sources only confirm Q4 2026 launch with exact mandatory date to be announced. ETIAS remains not yet operational as of June 2026.

ADDED EES note (2026-06-29): EU Entry/Exit System became fully operational 10 April 2026. EES records biometric data of all non-EU/EEA nationals at Schengen borders and is separate from ETIAS. Added to eta entry notes and visa_policy notes.

CONFIRMED (2026-06-29): No new countries added to or removed from Annex II of Regulation 2018/1806 in 2025-2026 beyond Vanuatu (removed January 2025 via Regulation 2025/11). Regulation 2025/2441 (November 2025) only strengthened the suspension mechanism, not the country list. All visa_free entries remain accurate.

CONFIRMED (2026-06-29): Belgium embassy Australia page (last updated June 24, 2026) confirms no new changes to the visa-free list for 2025-2026. The ETIAS fee of EUR 20 and Q4 2026 launch date remain as previously recorded.
gaps: Belgium's official IBZ website does not publish a standalone enumerated list of visa-free and visa-required nationalities; the authoritative reference is EU Regulation 2018/1806 on EUR-Lex.

The Armenia, Azerbaijan, and Belarus diplomatic passport exemptions listed in the prior record via EU visa facilitation agreements (OJ references) could not be confirmed in the EUR-Lex 2023XC0927(01) BNL/Belgium column table during this review - they have been removed pending re-verification against the full EU derogations table.
Regulation 2025/2441 (entered into force December 2025) updated the visa suspension mechanism rules but does not change the Annex II country list - no country-list impact confirmed.

The full diplomatic/service passport derogations table from EUR-Lex 52023XC0927(01) is a notification as of July 2023; a 2024 or 2025 updated version may exist but was not accessible for direct comparison.
Processing times for Researcher and ICT permits are not stated on the official IBZ pages consulted.
Dual citizenship rules for Belgium naturalization not confirmed from official source during this research session.

ETIAS launch date (Q4 2026) is confirmed but exact date within Q4 will be announced at least 6 months before launch. No specific mandatory enforcement sub-dates (beyond Q4 2026 launch) are officially confirmed as of June 2026. Prior revision notes referenced 'April 2027' and 'October 2027' milestones from a 2025 revised timeline document; these are no longer cited in current official EC pages and have been removed from the record.
Georgia diplomatic/service/official passport suspension (March 2026–March 2027) may be extended by up to 24 months if governance issues persist - check official EEAS/EC notices for updates.
Exact bilateral agreement dates for non-EU-framework diplomatic passport waivers are not published on the official IBZ or diplomatie.belgium.be pages.
No OCI (Overseas Citizen of India) cardholder exemption found in any official Belgian or EU source.

visa_types.name: Schengen Short-Stay Visa (Type C) - Tourism
visa_types.purpose: Allows foreign nationals to visit Belgium and other Schengen states for tourism, recreation, or visiting friends and family for up to 90 days within any 180-day period.
visa_types.notes: Standard Schengen C visa. Fee is EUR 90 for adults (12+), EUR 45 for children 6–12, free for children under 6. Single, double, or multiple-entry options available. Multiple-entry visas issued for 1, 2, or 5 years based on prior visa history. Valid across all 29 Schengen states. Medical travel insurance (minimum EUR 30,000 coverage) required. Must apply to country of primary destination.

visa_types.name: Schengen Short-Stay Visa (Type C) - Business
visa_types.purpose: Permits foreign nationals to enter Belgium and the Schengen area for business meetings, conferences, trade fairs, or other professional activities not amounting to employment, for up to 90 days in any 180-day period.
visa_types.notes: Same Type C Schengen visa framework as tourism. Fee EUR 90 (adults 12+). Paid work during the stay is not permitted under this category. Declaration of arrival required within 3 working days.

visa_types.name: Airport Transit Visa (Type A)
visa_types.purpose: Allows nationals of specified countries to transit through the international transit area of a Belgian airport without entering Schengen territory.
visa_types.notes: Required for nationals of approximately 25 countries including Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Iran, Iraq, Nigeria, Pakistan, Somalia, and others. Fee EUR 90 (adults 12+), EUR 45 (children 6–12), free for children under 6, students, researchers, and certain young non-profit representatives. Holders of valid residence permits from UK, Canada, Japan, and USA are exempt. Diplomatic and service/official passport holders from many countries are also exempt.

visa_types.name: Single Permit (Combined Work and Residence Authorization)
visa_types.purpose: Combined work and residence permit for third-country nationals intending to work in Belgium for more than 90 days under an employment contract.
visa_types.notes: Mandatory for all non-EU workers employed more than 90 days in Belgium since 3 January 2019. Application submitted by employer through regional one-stop counter portal. Region confirms receipt within 15 days; joint decision within 4 months (extendable). If no unfavorable decision within timeframe, authorization is automatically granted. Residence validity up to 5 years; renewal required at least 2 months before A card expiry. Holder also receives a national long-stay D visa from Belgian embassy to enter Belgium.

visa_types.name: EU Blue Card (Highly Qualified Worker - Single Permit B29)
visa_types.purpose: Combined work and residence permit for highly qualified third-country nationals employed by a Belgian employer under a salary above a specified threshold.
visa_types.notes: Identified with code B29 on visa/permit. Applied via Single Permit procedure by employer. EU Blue Card holders benefit from family reunification without proof of sufficient means and can combine EU-wide residency across Schengen states for long-term resident status (minimum 2 years in Belgium immediately before application).

visa_types.name: Intra-Corporate Transfer (ICT) Permit
visa_types.purpose: Permits managers, specialists, or trainee employees being transferred within a multinational enterprise to a Belgian entity for more than 90 days.
visa_types.notes: Separate procedure from the standard Single Permit. Employer must apply through regional authority. Holder also requires Belgian national long-stay D visa.

visa_types.name: Seasonal Worker Permit
visa_types.purpose: Authorizes third-country nationals to perform seasonal work in Belgium for a limited period exceeding 90 days.
visa_types.notes: Listed as a separate work authorization category by the Belgian Immigration Office. Regional authority determines eligibility and conditions. Holder also requires Belgian D visa.

visa_types.name: Au Pair / Youth Work Authorization
visa_types.purpose: Allows young third-country nationals to live with a host family in Belgium, participate in family life, and perform light household tasks while learning the local language and culture.
visa_types.notes: Listed as 'Au pair/Youth' work category by Belgian Immigration Office. Regional authority has jurisdiction. Holder also requires Belgian D visa for stays over 90 days.

visa_types.name: Trainee Work Authorization
visa_types.purpose: Permits third-country national trainees to perform on-the-job training in Belgium for more than 90 days within the framework of a training agreement.
visa_types.notes: Listed as 'Trainees' category by Belgian Immigration Office. Separate from the Single Permit standard procedure. Regional authority has jurisdiction.

visa_types.name: Volunteer Work Authorization
visa_types.purpose: Authorizes third-country nationals to carry out recognized volunteer work in Belgium for more than 90 days.
visa_types.notes: Listed as 'Volunteers' category by Belgian Immigration Office. Regional authority has jurisdiction. Holder requires Belgian D visa for stays exceeding 90 days.

visa_types.name: Professional Card (Self-Employed / Entrepreneur)
visa_types.purpose: Authorizes third-country nationals to conduct self-employed professional or commercial activities in Belgium for more than 90 days.
visa_types.notes: No fixed minimum investment amount set by federal law. Issued by the competent regional authority (Flanders, Wallonia, Brussels-Capital, or German-speaking Community). Applicant must then obtain a Belgian national long-stay D visa from the embassy/consulate. After 5 years of lawful residence, path to long-term resident status and potentially citizenship.

visa_types.name: Researcher Permit (Hosting Agreement - B13)
visa_types.purpose: Grants third-country national researchers the right to reside and conduct scientific research in Belgium for more than 90 days under a hosting agreement with a recognized Belgian research institution.
visa_types.notes: Designated B13 on visa/permit. Requires valid hosting agreement with recognized research organization. Regional employment authority declares admissibility first; decision issued within 60 days of complete file submission. Provisional 'Annex 49' document valid 45 days (renewable) issued while awaiting A card. Long-term mobility option available for researchers already authorized in another EU member state. Holder requires Belgian national long-stay D visa.

visa_types.name: Student Visa (Higher Education)
visa_types.purpose: Allows third-country nationals to reside in Belgium for more than 90 days to pursue degree-level studies at a recognized Belgian higher education institution.
visa_types.notes: Applies to studies leading to a degree recognized in Belgium. Holder must obtain Belgian national long-stay D visa from competent embassy/consulate before travel. Upon arrival, residence permit application made at municipal administration. Official fees not published on the IBZ page consulted.

visa_types.name: Student Visa (Secondary Education)
visa_types.purpose: Permits third-country nationals to reside in Belgium for more than 90 days to attend secondary school education.
visa_types.notes: Separate category from higher education. Holder requires Belgian D visa for stays over 90 days. Exact fee and processing times not published on official IBZ page.

visa_types.name: Orientation Year After Higher Studies
visa_types.purpose: Grants a one-year extension of residence to third-country national graduates of Belgian higher education institutions to seek employment or start a business.
visa_types.notes: Maximum 1 year, non-renewable as orientation year. At the end, applicant must convert to a work or entrepreneurship permit. Only available to graduates of recognized Belgian institutions.

visa_types.name: Working Holiday Programme Visa
visa_types.purpose: Allows young nationals of partner countries to holiday in Belgium for up to 12 months while supplementing their funds through incidental work.
visa_types.notes: Eligible countries: Australia, New Zealand, Canada, Taiwan, South Korea. Age limit: 18–30 at time of application. Financial requirement: minimum EUR 2,500 plus return ticket or funds therefor. Authorization valid 12 months and cannot be renewed. Leisure must be primary purpose; work is supplementary only. Requires Belgian national long-stay D visa before travel. Travel/health insurance required. Fee amount not specified on official page.

visa_types.name: Family Reunification Visa (with Belgian National)
visa_types.purpose: Permits eligible family members of Belgian nationals to reside in Belgium for more than 90 days for the purpose of family reunification.
visa_types.notes: Sponsor is a Belgian national. Processing time depends on nationality of the sponsoring Belgian resident. Applicant receives a national long-stay D visa and/or residence permit upon approval. Specific requirements and timelines available on IBZ sub-pages.

visa_types.name: Family Reunification Visa (with EU/EEA National)
visa_types.purpose: Permits eligible family members of EU or EEA nationals residing in Belgium to join them for the purpose of family reunification.
visa_types.notes: Sponsor is an EU/EEA/associated country national. Governed by EU Directive 2004/38 on freedom of movement for EU citizens. Specific requirements and timelines available on IBZ sub-pages.

visa_types.name: Family Reunification Visa (with Third-Country National)
visa_types.purpose: Permits eligible family members of legally resident third-country nationals to join them in Belgium for the purpose of family reunification.
visa_types.notes: Sponsor is a legally resident non-EU national. Stricter conditions apply including proof of stable resources and accommodation. Processing time depends on sponsor's nationality. Specific requirements on IBZ sub-pages.

visa_types.name: Family Reunification Visa (with International Protection Beneficiary)
visa_types.purpose: Permits family members of recognized refugees or subsidiary protection beneficiaries residing in Belgium to join them for family reunification.
visa_types.notes: Sponsor holds refugee status or subsidiary protection in Belgium. Specific conditions and timelines differ from standard family reunification. Applicant receives national long-stay D visa and/or residence permit upon approval.

visa_types.name: Diplomatic / Official Passport Visa Exemption
visa_types.purpose: Exempts holders of diplomatic, service/official, or special passports from certain countries from the Schengen visa requirement for short stays.
visa_types.notes: Applies to holders of diplomatic, service/official, and/or special passports from countries with bilateral visa waiver agreements with Belgium (or the Benelux), including Algeria, Bolivia, Cambodia, Cape Verde, China, Congo DRC, Cote d'Ivoire, Cuba, Ecuador, Egypt, Gabon, Gambia, Ghana, Guinea, India, Indonesia, Iran, Jamaica, Jordan, Kazakhstan, Malawi, Mongolia, Morocco, Oman, Pakistan, Philippines, Qatar, Senegal, South Africa, Thailand, Tunisia, Turkey, and others. Scope (diplomatic only vs. diplomatic+service vs. diplomatic+service+special) varies by country per EUR-Lex 52023XC0927(01) BNL column.

visa_types.name: National Long-Stay Visa (Type D)
visa_types.purpose: Belgian national visa authorizing third-country nationals to enter Belgium and take up long-term residence (more than 90 days) for purposes such as work, study, family reunification, or self-employment.
visa_types.notes: All long-stay categories (work, study, family, research, self-employment, etc.) require a Type D national long-stay visa obtained from the Belgian embassy/consulate in the applicant's country of residence before travel. Upon arrival applicants register at the municipal administration and apply for a residence permit (A card or equivalent). All nationalities are subject to the D visa requirement for long stays regardless of short-stay exemption status.
